description: Respiratory emergency cases for Resident Life simulator - real clinical scenarios with real lab/imaging results

patients.name: Mr. Youssef
patients.complaint: Severe dyspnea for 2 hours, unable to speak in full sentences. Known COPD, on home O2 2L. Productive cough with green sputum for 3 days.
patients.triageNote: 68M, severe respiratory distress, speaking 2-3 words, accessory muscle use, SpO2 82% on home O2, febrile 38.8°C
patients.correctDiagnosis: Acute COPD Exacerbation with Acute-on-Chronic Hypercapnic Respiratory Failure

result.finding: pH 7.22, pCO2 82, pO2 48, HCO3 35, Lactate 2.8, SaO2 80%
result.interpretation: Severe acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis (pH <7.25 = NIV indicated). Chronic compensation (HCO3 35) suggests chronic CO2 retention. Severe hypoxemia.

result.finding: Hyperinflated lungs with flattened diaphragms. Increased AP diameter. No focal consolidation. No pneumothorax. No pulmonary edema. Cardiomegaly absent.
result.interpretation: COPD changes without pneumonia, heart failure, or pneumothorax

result.finding: Sinus tachycardia 118. Right axis deviation. P-pulmonale (peaked P in II >2.5mm). Right ventricular hypertrophy. No acute ST changes.
result.interpretation: Right heart strain from chronic pulmonary hypertension (cor pulmonale). No acute MI.

result.finding: WBC 18.2, Neutrophils 88%, Hb 16.8, Hct 52%, Plt 320
result.interpretation: Leukocytosis with neutrophil predominance suggests bacterial infection. Polycythemia from chronic hypoxemia (Hb 16.8, Hct 52%).

result.finding: CRP: 145 mg/L
result.interpretation: Markedly elevated - supports bacterial infection triggering exacerbation

result.finding: Gram stain: Many WBCs, Gram-negative rods. Culture pending 48-72h.
result.interpretation: Likely Haemophilus influenzae or Moraxella catarrhalis - common COPD pathogens

result.finding: BiPAP initiated: IPAP 16/EPAP 6, FiO2 0.5. After 30 min: pH 7.31, pCO2 68, SpO2 93%. Work of breathing significantly decreased.
result.interpretation: Good response to NIV. pH improving. CO2 clearing. Avoids intubation.

result.finding: Wheezing decreased. Peak flow improved from 80 to 140 L/min.
result.interpretation: Bronchodilator responsive component to exacerbation

result.finding: Systemic corticosteroid administered. Peak effect 4-6 hours. Reduces inflammation and speeds recovery.
result.interpretation: Steroids reduce treatment failure by 50% in COPD exacerbation (REDUCE trial)

result.finding: Antibiotics initiated for purulent sputum + increased dyspnea + increased sputum volume (Anthonisen criteria: 3/3 = antibiotics indicated)
result.interpretation: Targets common COPD pathogens: H. influenzae, M. catarrhalis, S. pneumoniae

result.finding: WARNING: Intubation reserved for NIV failure. Mortality 30-50% in intubated COPD patients.
result.interpretation: Only intubate if: pH <7.25 despite NIV, GCS <8, respiratory arrest, or inability to tolerate NIV

treatmentPathway.wrongActionsConsequence: Early intubation in COPD carries high mortality. NIV is first-line for pH >7.25.

criticalErrors.consequence: Intubating without NIV trial in COPD with pH >7.25 is inappropriate. NIV success rate 80-85%. Intubation mortality 30-50%.

stages.event: pH dropping below 7.20. Severe hypercapnia. GCS declining.
CO2 narcosis. Patient obtunded. Impending respiratory arrest.
Respiratory arrest. Cardiac arrest if not intubated immediately.
NIV working partially. pH 7.28. Still tachypneic.
Gradual improvement. Needs bronchodilators and steroids.
Slow recovery. Antibiotics indicated for purulent sputum.
Good response. pH 7.31. SpO2 93% on BiPAP.
Improving. Considering transition to nasal cannula.
Stable on 2L NC. pH 7.36. Admission to respiratory ward.

learningPoints.missedDiagnosis: If you thought it was heart failure: Hyperinflated lungs + no crackles + no cardiomegaly = COPD, not HF. BNP may be mildly elevated in cor pulmonale.
learningPoints.keyTeaching: NIV reduces intubation by 50% and mortality by 40% in COPD exacerbation. Indications: pH <7.35, pCO2 >45. Contraindications: GCS <8, respiratory arrest, unable to protect airway.
learningPoints.guideline: GOLD Guidelines 2023: Bronchodilators + systemic corticosteroids + antibiotics (if 3 Anthonisen criteria met) + NIV for acute hypercapnic respiratory failure.

patients.name: Mrs. Amal
patients.complaint: Sudden onset severe left-sided pleuritic chest pain and acute dyspnea while running. Tall, thin build. No prior medical history. Non-smoker.
patients.triageNote: 34F, acute pleuritic pain + dyspnea, tall thin female, SpO2 91% on RA, HR 118, RR 28

result.finding: Large left-sided pneumothorax (approximately 60%). Visible pleural line. No mediastinal shift. Lung markings absent in left hemithorax. Right lung normal.
result.interpretation: Large primary spontaneous pneumothorax without tension physiology. No mediastinal shift = no tension pneumothorax.

result.finding: Sinus tachycardia 118. Normal axis. No ST changes. No S1Q3T3 pattern. No right heart strain.
result.interpretation: No evidence of PE or cardiac ischemia
Mild hypoxemia. No hypercapnia. Normal acid-base.

result.finding: Absent lung sliding on left anterior chest. Barcode/stratosphere sign on M-mode. Lung point identified laterally. Right lung normal sliding.
result.interpretation: Ultrasound confirms pneumothorax with high sensitivity (95%). Lung point sign = 100% specific.

result.finding: WARNING: Needle decompression indicated ONLY for tension pneumothorax. This patient has NO tension (no mediastinal shift, no hypotension, no tracheal deviation).
result.interpretation: Unnecessary needle decompression causes iatrogenic injury and converts simple pneumothorax to open pneumothorax

possibleActions.name: Chest Tube Insertion (Large-Bore, 5th ICS, Anterior Axillary Line)

result.finding: Large-bore chest tube placed. Lung re-expanded. Air leak present. Underwater seal drainage functioning.
result.interpretation: Large-bore chest tube is definitive treatment for large pneumothorax. However, for primary spontaneous pneumothorax, small-bore catheter or pigtail may suffice.

result.finding: Pigtail catheter placed under local anesthesia. Lung fully re-expanded within 30 minutes. Minimal discomfort. Heimlich valve allows ambulation.
result.interpretation: Small-bore catheter is first-line for primary spontaneous pneumothorax. Less painful, equally effective. Allows outpatient management.

result.finding: High-flow O2 15L via non-rebreather. Nitrogen washout accelerates pleural air absorption (rate: 1.25% lung volume per day on O2 vs 0.25% on room air).
result.interpretation: Observation acceptable ONLY for small pneumothorax (<2cm or <15% lung volume) in stable patient

treatmentPathway.wrongActionsConsequence: Needle decompression in non-tension pneumothorax causes unnecessary harm

criticalErrors.consequence: Needle decompression is ONLY for tension pneumothorax (hypotension + tracheal deviation + absent breath sounds). This patient has NO tension signs. Iatrogenic open pneumothorax created.

criticalErrors.whenSizeIsLarge: Observation for large pneumothorax (>2cm) delays definitive treatment. Patient remains symptomatic and at risk for tension.

stages.event: Increasing dyspnea. SpO2 dropping to 88%.
Pneumothorax enlarging. Beginning mediastinal shift.
Developing tension pneumothorax: hypotension, tracheal deviation, distended neck veins.
Cardiac arrest from obstructive shock.
Pigtail catheter placed. Lung re-expanding. Pain resolving.
CXR confirms full lung expansion. SpO2 98% on RA.
Stable. Can be discharged with Heimlich valve and follow-up in 2-3 days.

learningPoints.missedDiagnosis: If you thought it was PE: Acute onset pleuritic pain in young thin tall male/female = pneumothorax until proven otherwise. CXR is diagnostic. Lung ultrasound has 95% sensitivity.
learningPoints.keyTeaching: Primary spontaneous pneumothorax: Tall thin young patients, no underlying lung disease. Treatment: Small pneumothorax (<2cm) = observation + O2. Large (>2cm) or symptomatic = small-bore catheter/pigtail + Heimlich valve. Large-bore chest tube only if pigtail fails.
learningPoints.guideline: BTS Pleural Disease Guideline: Small-bore catheters are first-line for primary spontaneous pneumothorax. Needle aspiration equally effective. Large-bore chest tubes reserved for secondary pneumothorax or trauma.

patients.name: Mr. Tariq
patients.complaint: Fever 39.5°C, productive cough with rusty-colored sputum, right-sided pleuritic chest pain, and rigors for 2 days. Previously healthy. Smoker 20 pack-years.
patients.triageNote: 52M, febrile 39.5°C, HR 112, BP 100/65, RR 30, SpO2 89% on RA, crackles right lower zone
patients.correctDiagnosis: Severe Community-Acquired Pneumonia (Right Lower Lobe), CURB-65 = 2 (Confusion: 0, Urea: pending, RR ≥30: 1, BP <90/60: 0, Age ≥65: 0) = Consider admission

result.finding: Right lower lobe dense consolidation with air bronchograms. No pleural effusion. No cavitation. Left lung clear. Cardiac size normal.
result.interpretation: Classic lobar pneumonia - right lower lobe consolidation. Air bronchograms confirm alveolar filling.

result.finding: pH 7.44, pCO2 32, pO2 58, HCO3 22, Lactate 3.2, SaO2 89%
result.interpretation: Type 1 respiratory failure (hypoxemic). Respiratory alkalosis from tachypnea (pCO2 32). Elevated lactate suggests early sepsis.

result.finding: WBC 22.4, Neutrophils 92% with left shift and toxic granulation, Bands 12%, Hb 14.2, Plt 180
result.interpretation: Marked leukocytosis with severe left shift (bands 12%) and toxic granulation = severe bacterial infection. Sepsis physiology.

result.finding: CRP: 285 mg/L, Procalcitonin: 12.5 ng/mL
result.interpretation: CRP >200 = severe inflammation. PCT >2.0 = bacterial infection confirmed. PCT >10 = high risk of severe sepsis/septic shock.

result.finding: Cultures drawn pre-antibiotics. Preliminary report in 24h: Gram-positive cocci in pairs. Final: Streptococcus pneumoniae, penicillin-sensitive.
result.interpretation: Streptococcus pneumoniae = most common cause of community-acquired pneumonia

result.finding: Good quality specimen (>25 WBC, <10 epithelial cells/LPF). Gram-positive diplococci. Culture pending.
result.interpretation: Good sputum quality. Gram-positive diplococci = likely Streptococcus pneumoniae

result.finding: Pneumococcal urinary antigen: POSITIVE. Legionella: Negative.
result.interpretation: Confirms Streptococcus pneumoniae as pathogen

result.finding: O2 4L via nasal cannula. SpO2 improved to 95%.
result.interpretation: Hypoxemia corrected. Target SpO2 94-98% for non-COPD patients.

result.finding: Antibiotics started within 2 hours of arrival (meets SEP-1 measure). Covering typical CAP pathogens (S. pneumo, atypicals, H. influenzae).
result.interpretation: Ceftriaxone + Azithromycin = standard empiric therapy for severe CAP. Time to antibiotics <4 hours reduces mortality.

result.finding: 2L Lactated Ringer's bolus initiated. BP improved to 118/72. Lactate clearance pending in 2 hours.
result.interpretation: Fluid resuscitation for severe sepsis. 30mL/kg crystalloid is standard initial bolus. Reassess after.

treatmentPathway.wrongActionsConsequence: Delay in antibiotics increases mortality by 8% per hour in septic shock

criticalErrors.consequence: Every hour delay in antibiotics in severe sepsis increases mortality by 7-8%. SEP-1 measure requires antibiotics within 3 hours.

stages.event: Worsening hypoxemia. SpO2 84%. Increasing work of breathing.
Developing septic shock: BP 78/45, Lactate 5.8, altered mental status.
ARDS developing. Bilateral infiltrates. Refractory hypoxemia.
Antibiotics working but still hypotensive. Needs fluids.
Lactate 3.8. BP 95/60. Partial response.
Slow improvement. ICU admission for monitoring.
O2 saturation 95%. BP 115/70. Responding to fluids + antibiotics.
Fever down to 38.0°C. Clinical improvement.
Stable. Admission to medical ward. Continue IV antibiotics.

learningPoints.missedDiagnosis: If you thought it was bronchitis: Bronchitis has normal CXR. This CXR shows dense consolidation = pneumonia. Fever 39.5°C + rigors + rusty sputum = classic pneumococcal pneumonia.
learningPoints.keyTeaching: CURB-65 score: Confusion (0), Urea >7 (1 if elevated), RR ≥30 (1), BP <90/60 (0), Age ≥65 (0) = Score 2. Score 2 = consider hospital admission. Severe CAP criteria: 1 major (septic shock, need for mechanical ventilation) OR 3 minor (RR ≥30, PaO2/FiO2 <250, multilobar infiltrates, confusion, BUN >20, WBC <4, Plt <100, hypothermia <36°C).
learningPoints.guideline: IDSA/ATS CAP Guidelines: Empiric therapy for severe CAP = beta-lactam (ceftriaxone) + macrolide (azithromycin) OR beta-lactam + respiratory fluoroquinolone. Add vancomycin if MRSA risk. Blood cultures BEFORE antibiotics.
